title: St. Dionysius the Areopagite, St. Timothy of Alexandria, St. Puplius of Athens & St. Sabinus of Egypt
narrative:
St. Dionysius the Areopagite, St. Timothy Pope of Alexandria, St. Puplius of Athens, and St. Sabinus of Egypt

On the thirteenth of Megabit, the holy Church meditates upon the relationship between philosophical wisdom and Christian confession, between the city of Athens and the city of God, between the academy and the martyrium. Saint Dionysius the Areopagite, the member of the Athenian court who was converted by Paul's sermon on the Areopagus and became, by tradition, the first bishop of Athens, stands as the supreme figure of this relationship: a philosopher who found in Paul's proclamation of the Unknown God the answer to the question that Greek philosophy had been asking for centuries. The tradition that identifies the first bishop of Athens with the later author of the mystical theological corpus attributed to Dionysius makes him the patron of the entire enterprise of negative theology — the theology that knows God primarily through the recognition of what God is not — which runs as a deep current through both the Greek and the Ethiopian theological traditions.

Saint Timothy of Alexandria returns in the Megabit cycle as the governing authority of the Alexandrian tradition in the turbulent decades of the post-Chalcedonian controversy, where the great Alexandrian heritage of Dionysius and Clement and Origen and Athanasius had to be navigated through an institutional crisis that threatened to tear the church apart. Timothy's achievement was conservative in the best sense: he preserved what had been handed to him while adapting its expression to the demands of a changed situation, neither surrendering the tradition nor allowing it to become a rigid defence against all renewal. The Ethiopian Church, which received its theological formation through Alexandria, honours him as a trustworthy transmitter.

Saint Puplius of Athens is this day's figure of discovery and one of the least-known witnesses in the entire Ethiopian Synaxarium: a monk and bishop who served the Christian community of Athens in the second or third century, whose brief biographical notice preserves almost nothing beyond his name, his see, and a quality of life characterised by extraordinary prayer and fasting. The tradition notes that Puplius was said to be the successor of Dionysius the Areopagite in the see of Athens, which if true makes him the inheritor of the most philosophically distinguished community in the early Church and the person responsible for nurturing a community that lived at the intersection of the Christian faith and the entire achievement of classical Greek thought. That we know so little about him is perhaps fitting: philosophy itself ends in the recognition of what it cannot know.

Saint Sabinus of Egypt is a martyr of the Diocletianic persecution whose story the tradition preserves in one of its most moving episodes of providential concealment. Sabinus was a bishop of the region of Hermopolis who, when the persecution began, hid himself in the marshes of the Nile Delta with a group of his clergy and faithful, sustained by the food that the local poor brought to them at great personal risk. He was eventually betrayed for a reward by a beggar whom he had personally been in the habit of feeding, was arrested, tried, and drowned in the Nile. The Ethiopian Synaxarium preserves this story as a meditation on the strange economy of grace: that the bishop who fed the hungry was destroyed by one of the hungry he fed, and that this too is a form of the Cross — the giving that exhausts itself in giving even to those who will use the gift for betrayal. Amen.
